text: The surgeon uses sharp scissors to release adhesions, applying adequate counter-traction to expose the tissue plane. Minor bleeding is observed but is not actively controlled unless it becomes significant.
The surgeon applies diathermy to the adhesions, maintaining a safe distance from the bowel to keep the surgical field clean.

so main important thing step in doing any ventral hernia repair is a desolation if there is any enterotomy accidental enterotomy the entire plan will get collapsed then we will not be able to place mess because of fear of infection and you can see this patient has got multiple defects So when you see momentum we are happy but if you see bubble we have to be very careful. Even here you see immediately behind the momentum is bubble. So we should always think before doing each and every cut that there is a bubble lobe sitting behind it. That has to be there.

The surgeon releases the omentum adherent to the bowel, revealing no plane between the abdominal wall and bowel loop. To prevent intestinal injury, the surgeon dissects one layer above, removing the peritoneum along with the bowel loop. A pre-peritoneal pad of fat is visible, and occasionally a portion of the rectus sheath is also removed. A patch of peritoneum is clearly seen being removed from the abdominal wall.
The surgeon applies counter traction, revealing another loop of bowel as adhesions are carefully released.

Using a 30-degree telescope and sharp scissors, the surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac, dividing the attachments of the omentum to prevent necrosis and sinus formation. The dissection is performed in multiple directions to ensure safe removal of the sac's contents.

The surgeon identifies and removes the entire hernia content before placing the mesh. A flattened bowel loop is visible, and multiple defects are noted. The surgeon distinguishes between the bowel and the hernia sac, confidently dividing the hernia sac. The final adhesion is released, completing the adhesion release process.
